title: Bak--Tang--Wiesenfeld model for various topologies and ranges of interaction
abstract: In this paper, the Bak--Tang--Wiesenfeld model for various substrate topologies and a variety of neighborhoods is reconsidered. With computer simulation, we study the distribution of avalanche sizes. Using the Z-score we confirm that independently of the substrate topology and the range of neighborhood, the exponent that governs the power law of the probability distribution of the size of avalanches is the same and approximately equal 1.208(39). However, this requires a smartly chosen number of deposited grains in relation to the linear size of the system.
summary: This paper investigates the universality of the critical exponent in the Bak-Tang-Wiesenfeld sandpile model across different lattice structures and interaction rules. Using extensive computer simulations, the authors demonstrate that the exponent governing avalanche size distributions remains constant, suggesting a robust, universal critical behavior independent of the specific underlying geometry, provided system parameters are scaled correctly.
main_problem: The paper reconsiders the Bak-Tang-Wiesenfeld (BTW) model to study how varying substrate topologies and interaction ranges affect the power-law distribution of avalanche sizes.
main_result: The critical exponent governing the avalanche size distribution ($	au \approx 1.208(39)$) is found to be statistically universal, remaining constant regardless of the substrate topology or interaction range, provided the deposition ratio ($N/L^2$) is correctly chosen.
method: The study employs computer simulations (Monte Carlo) to model the sandpile dynamics and analyzes the resulting probability distribution $P(s)$ using power-law fitting and Z-score statistical tests.
model_or_system: The system is the BTW sandpile model, where grains are added to a lattice (square, triangular, or honeycomb) until an avalanche occurs, causing material to fall to neighbors based on defined interaction ranges (nearest vs. next-nearest neighbors).
key_observables: The power-law exponent $	au$ governing $P(s) \propto s^{-	au}$, and the statistical consistency of $	au$ across different lattice setups.
important_parameters: Substrate topologies (sq, tr, hc), interaction ranges (1NN, 2NN), and the ratio of deposited particles to system size ($N/L^2$).
main_assumptions: The observation of the power law is highly dependent on maintaining a specific, reasonably chosen ratio between the number of deposited particles ($N$) and the system size ($L^2$).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the various lattices and neighborhoods, show the log-log binned probability distributions $P(s)$ for different setups, and Tables summarize the calculated exponent $	au$ and statistical comparison values.
paper_structure: The paper systematically tests the BTW model across multiple lattice types and interaction rules, using simulations to fit the power-law exponent $	au$ and statistically confirming its independence from the specific lattice geometry when system parameters are controlled.
why_interesting: While focused on classical sandpile dynamics, the study of universal exponents ($	au$) in non-equilibrium critical phenomena is a core concept in statistical mechanics, relevant to understanding phase transitions and critical behavior in complex quantum many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (a)–(b) Square, (c)–(d) triangular and (e)–(f) honeycomb lattices with marked neighborhoods in the first (a), (c), (e) and the first plus the second (b), (d), (f) coordination zone. As honeycomb lattice does not belong to the Bravais’ lattices, the sites in the first coordination zone are different in odd and even rows and columns
FIG. 2: Computerized version of lattices shown in Figure 1 mapped into square lattice: (a) sq-1; (b) sq-1,2; (c)

Figure 2(c)). The honeycomb lattice can be converted to a brick-wall lattice representation (see Figures 2(e) and 2(f)). Unfortunately, as honeycomb lattice is ex- cluded from Bravais lattices, their one-dimensional rep- resentation must be split to odd and even numbers of rows and columns. An example of Fortran implemen- tation of hc-1,2 neighborhood is presented in Listing 3 in Appendix B. Additionally, Listings 1 and 2 in Ap- pendix B show more obvious implementations of sq-1,2 and tr-1,2 neighborhoods (also as Fortran subroutines), respectively.
FIG. 3: Probability distribution P(s) of avalanches size s logarithmically binned for L = 103 and N = 108 (for raw data look at orange symbols in Figure 4 presented in Appendix A)

FIG. 4: Probability distribution P(s) of sizes of avalanches s for various lattices and neighborhoods: (a) sq-1, (b) sq-1,2, (c) tr-1, (d) tr-1,2, (e) hc-1, (f) hc-1,2. Various number N of deposited particles, fixed number of sites L2 = 106
